1_guion_sesion_1.title: SESSION 1: MICROBLAZE PLATFORM FOR PETALINUX
1_guion_sesion_1.content:
SESSION 1: MICROBLAZE PLATFORM FOR PETALINUX
In this first session we are learning on how to generate from zero (to hero) a Petalinux system using the AMD Software for FPGA development. The platform will be based on the classic Microblaze architecture with many peripherals attached to it, as we have seen in the theory sessions.


                  
                  Deliverables 
                  
                

At the end of this session you will have to deliver three things:

The Question paper with all the questions answered. They will be developed through the lab session
The XSA file from Vivado
The screenshot of the address editor



PART 1: BUILDING THE MICROBLAZE PLATFORM IN VIVADO
For this section, only Vivado 2024.1 software is needed.

Download the archived project from PoliformaT and unzip it in a folder of your choice.



                  
                  Warning
                  
                

UNZIP THE FOLDER IN A PATH THAT IS NOT VERY LONG AS WINDOWS CAN COMPLAIN ABOUT THIS!



Open Vivado and click on Open Project.
Go to the folder where the project was unzipped and open the .xpr file to open the Vivado project.
Follow the instructions in the next sections to complete the Vivado project, making it ready for booting up Linux.

Including QSPI Controller
The project is almost completed, only one IP block is left: The QSPI controller.


                  
                  Question 1 
                  
                

Why is the QSPI controller needed?



Click on Open Block Design in the IP Integrator section.



Right click in an empty part of the Diagram screen and click on Add IP.



Select AXI Quad SPI.
Double click on the IP and configure the IP as shown in the photo.




                  
                  Question 2 
                  
                

How is it known that the Mode must be Quad and the Slave Device Spansion?
Tip: Look at the Genesys 2 reference manual for the QSPI flash model.



Connect the IP to the following ports. For that just hold click on the port and draw the cursor to the destination port where you want to connect the first port to:

AXI_LITE → M02_AXI of AXI Interconnect
ext_spi_clk → ui_clk from MIG 7 Series
s_axi_aclk → ui_clk from MIG 7 Series
s_axi_aresetn → peripheral_aresetn from rst_mig_7series_0_100M



Analyze Microblaze processor and memory map
In this section we are going to edit the Microblaze processor:

Double click on the Microblaze processor.



                  
                  Question 3 
                  
                

What is needed for the Microblaze to run Linux?



Go to the cache page and increase the cache to 64kB.



                  
                  Question 4 
                  
                

Check the Base Address and High Address values in the Instruction and Data Cache.
Where are these values drawn from? What do they represent?



Go to the Address Editor tab.
Right click and in the contextual menu click on Assign All. This will assign physical memory spaces in the addressing space of the CPU.



                  
                  Question 5 
                  
                

Change the Available internal memory of the CPU from 8kB to 64kB.
What effect will this cause in our design? What is it useful for?



Make a screenshot expanding all the elements from the address editor and attach it in the PoliformaT task.

Editing Constraints
Add the following constraints to the XDC file. The XDC file is in the Sources section, Constraints folder.

set_property BITSTREAM.CONFIG.SPI_BUSWIDTH 4 [current_design]
set_property BITSTREAM.GENERAL.COMPRESS TRUE [current_design]


                  
                  Question 6 
                  
                

What do the previous commands do?


Select bootup method

Run Synthesis of the design.
Tools → Edit Device Properties → Configuration modes → Master SPIx4.


Generate bitstream

Click on Generate Bitstream to go through the whole build process.
Open Implemented Design.
Click on Report Utilization and check the utilization report to see which FPGA resources were used.
Attach a screenshot of the Summary section of the Utilization Report.

Generate XSA file

Go to File → Export → Export Hardware.
Check Include Bitstream.
Select where you want to save the XSA file.
Click Next several times and Finish.



                  
                  Question 7 
                  
                

What does this file include?
Tip: Open it using 7zip.


PART 2: GENERATING AND BOOTING PETALINUX IMAGE
In this section, we are going to build a custom Linux image using the Vivado XSA. The software that will be used are the Petalinux Tools 2024.1.

Connect to the Linux VM through Windows Remote Desktop application using the IP address and the username and password provided by the teacher.
Open a terminal in Linux and run the script that configures the environment for Petalinux building commands.
source /opt/petalinuxTools/2024/settings.sh

Create the Petalinux project. This project will be the center of all the changes made in the software for building the Linux platform and software that comes with it.

petalinux-create -t project -n linux_mb_2024 --template microblaze


                  
                  Question 8 
                  
                

What options are there for the template argument?
Check UG1144 Petalinux Tools Reference Guide.



We have to tell Petalinux which hardware we have so that the correct software and drivers components are built. As the in the FPGA we can include any hardware that our imagination (and our Verilog coding skills) allow us, Petalinux Tools need to know exactly what we included and what not.

petalinux-config --get-hw-description <folder where the xsa was copied> --silentconfig
Configuring the linux kernel

There is one hardware added: the Ethernet link that requires additional drivers because there is a PHY chip in the board that negotiates the link. This is a Realtek chip that has their own set of drivers.
Run the following command to enter into the kernel customization menu:

petalinux-config -c kernel


You will be presented with a Kconfig menu that allows to configure every single part of the Linux kernel, to tune it to the developer needs. Go to the corresponding section by using the arrow keys and the Enter key and check the Realtek PHY support using the y key:

> Device Drivers > Network device support > PHY Device support and infrastructure




Go to the following directory:
<petalinux-project-folder>/components/yocto/layers/poky/meta/recipes-core/libxcrypt


Open libxcrypt.inc and change the SRCREV variable to "55ea777e8d567e5e86ffac917c28815ac54cc341"




                  
                  Info
                  
                

This last step is made to fix a bug in one of the Yocto recipes used in Petalinux Tools 2024.1. What it does is to change the commit SHA where it downloads the source code from to compile the libxcrypt library. It basically updates the library from version 4.4.30 to the latest one, where this bug has already been fixed.


Boot flow, configuring offsets
Let’s analyze the boot flow. Petalinux will boot from the QSPI flash, using the bootflow that we saw in the slides. For that it will need several files and each one of them must be in a specific offset that we are configuring the Petalinux boot flow to find that file. To configure those offsets the following steps must be made:

Go to the main Petalinux configuration screen:

petalinux-config

Navigate through the menu to the qspi offset section:

 → Subsystem Hardware Settings → Flash Settings

Configure the offset using the following options:




                  
                  Question 9 
                  
                

What does each partition represent? Fill the table below.
How is the required size of each partition computed?
































PartitionSizeDescriptionspi0-fpga0xB00000spi0-boot0xA0000spi0-bootenv0x20000spi0-kernel0xC00000
Editing the device tree
Including a new driver means that we need to configure it. This is done through the device tree, as explained in the theory slides. For this lab session we are going to configure the Realtek PHY included in the Genesys 2:

In the project folder, go to project-spec/meta-user/recipes-bsp/device-tree/files.
Open the system-user.dtsi file.
Add the following lines of text to the file:

&axi_ethernet_0 {
    phy-handle = <&phy0>;
    axi_ethernet_0_mdio: mdio {
        #address-cells = <1>;
        #size-cells = <0>;
        phy0: ethernet-phy@0 {
            device_type = "ethernet-phy";
            compatible = "ethernet-phy-id001c.c915";
            reg = <1>;
        };
    };
};


                  
                  Question 10 
                  
                

What does the compatible property and the reg property represent?




                  
                  Question 11 
                  
                

Where can you get the information of what to write in the compatible and the reg property?



Build just the device-tree to check for any Syntax Errors that might have been committed.
petalinux-build -c device-tree




                  
                  Info
                  
                

The command in the previous step builds just the device-tree component, through the -c parameter.


Bootup Petalinux
The project is completely configured. Now let’s build an image and flash it into the Genesys 2 board.

The command for building Petalinux images is petalinux-build. It takes very long to run so we are going to use a prebuilt image.
For completeness, there is another command, called petalinux-package, that takes all the outputs from the previous one and puts it inside an MCS (Memory Configuration System) file. This file is used to initialize the QSPI flash inside the Genesys 2 board with all the required files for booting Linux.




                  
                  Question 12 
                  
                

Open the MCS with a text editor. What do you find inside?


PONER AQUI TODAS LAS CAPTURAS

Open Vivado 2024.1 and go to Hardware Manager.
Click on Open Target → Auto Connect.

Right click on the FPGA device found (kcu7) and click on Add Configuration Memory Device.

In the window, select the correct memory device: s25fl256xxxxxx0.

Click OK and say Yes to the message prompting you to program the memory device.

Check the MCS option in the next window and add the boot.mcs boot file that you have to download from PoliformaT.

Click OK and wait for the device to be programmed.
Once programmed, open Putty and select the serial terminal with the following options:


Serial Line: COMx (check in Windows device manager)
Speed: 115200
Bits: 8
Stop bits: 1
Parity: None
Flow Control: OFF



Turn off and back on the board and wait for Petalinux to boot.

Running some commands in Petalinux
Once in the login prompt, enter the petalinux user and then write a new password when asked by the system.


                  
                  Question 13 
                  
                

Run the following commands and copy and paste the output:

cat /proc/cpuinfo
cat /proc/meminfo
What kind of information does each one of these commands show?

2_guion_sesion_2.title: SESSION 2: SOFTWARE COMPONENTS OF PETALINUX
2_guion_sesion_2.content:
SESSION 2: SOFTWARE COMPONENTS OF PETALINUX
In this session we are learning on how to take advantage of the system we have built with our own hands in the previous session.
Linux is not just some piece of static fancy software that is running on a computer so that you can show off to your friends…
In this session, we are going to learn how to edit every single piece of code, used from the buildup of the kernel, going through the bootloader process to the final end-user software that will be running in our platform.
Also, if time is on our side and the Fortune Goddess smiles upon us, we can also learn how to add the end software after having been tested, to a prebuilt Linux image so that the end user doesn’t have to worry about downloading any extra packages.


                  
                  Deliverables 
                  
                

At the end of this session you will have to deliver three things:

The source code of the GPIO application
The .elf file, ready to be executed in the Genesys 2



Patching the bootloader
Before entering into coding a new application for ourselves, we will enter into the world of patching, that is, picking some code and changing it to our liking to add new features or fix nasty bugs.
In Petalinux Tools, or in Yocto in general, there is the devtool concept, where the tools will download a specific source code of a software and will leave it inside the project locally so that you can change whichever part of the code you want.
Then, after the changes have been made, when issuing the petalinux-build, instead of pulling the source code from the repository, the local copy with your changes will be used.
This is a very convenient way to test small changes done to other people’s software without having to pull their repositories in a separate directory, all of this is already integrated inside Yocto.
We are going to patch the bootloader of Microblaze, defined as the first piece of software that the Microblaze CPU runs when booting up the system. This bootloader is a very small piece of software that must fit into the BlockRAMs, so having as little code as possible and as optimized as possible for our use case is crucial:

Pull the source code using petalinux-devtool:
petalinux-devtool modify fs-boot

The previous command will download the source code in components/yocto/workspace/sources/fs-boot. Go to that folder.
There, you will find the source code of the first stage bootloader. The main() function of this bootloader is in components/yocto/workspace/sources/fs-boot/lib/sw_apps/mba_fs_boot. Open the file fs-boot.c.
Make some modification, like for example adding a printf at the beginning of the main().
Now, we could build the Petalinux image and test this change, no more steps are needed. However, we are going to complete the flow now.
The pull that devtool does gives a repository as a result, meaning that when you make changes you can commit them. Each one of these commits will be transformed into a .patch file.
git add -A
git commit -m "My Patched changes"

Once the commits are done, run the other relevant petalinux-devtool command variant:
petalinux-devtool finish fs-boot <absolute route to the project>/project-spec/meta-user

Now, inside project-spec/meta-user/recipes-bsp/fs-boot, there should be a .patch file for each commit you made. Find in the following link an example of a patch file.

Coding applications in Vitis
AMD provides an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for their FPGA solutions, where all the tools needed for building and debugging software are included. This software is called Vitis.
In this section, you will code an application for the Microblaze CPU that you built in the previous session. The statement is as follows:
To code this application, please take a look into how GPIO works in Linux through sysfs.

We are going to need both the XSA from Vivado 2024.1 and an SDK that contains the Microblaze cross-compiler and the sysroot. Download both the XSA and SDK.zip from PoliformaT.



                  
                  About sysroot 
                  
                

It is a directory which is considered to be the root directory for the purpose of locating headers and libraries. It is a directory that contains a full Linux rootfs made for the target architecture of the CPU, so it contains all the libraries (.so) and headers (.h) files specially made for that architecture.




                  
                  Danger
                  
                

The sysroot is completely needed as we CANNOT use the libraries included in the host OS because they are made for a different architecture (x86).




Extract SDK.zip in a folder of your choice.


Open Vitis Classic 2024.1.


Choose the workspace you want to work in (recommended to leave the default one).



Create a platform project.





                  
                  About a platform project 
                  
                

The platform project is the project that contains all the hardware information of the board and the type of OS it is running (Linux, FreeRTOS, or standalone). This information is gathered from the XSA generated by Vivado. For Linux, more information is required, such as the sysroot.




Select the XSA that you downloaded previously.



When the Software specification loads, select in the Operating system dropdown Linux.



Click Finish to create the platform project.


Click on platform.spr and then go to the Linux on microblaze_0 section.


Search for Sysroot and click the Browse button next to it.



Go to the folder where you unzipped the SDK.zip file and select <sdk-directory>/sysroots/microblazeel-v11.0-bs-cmp-re-mh-div-xilinx-linux.



Build the platform project by clicking on the hammer icon.



Create the application project.



Select the platform project created before in the following screen:



Click Next and input a name for the system project.


Click Next again until you reach the following screen. Select as template the Linux Hello World.



Click Finish to create the application project.


Build the application project using the Debug profile.



Once built, go to the Run button dropdown list and click on Run Configurations….



Create a new configuration of type TCF (Single Application Debug).





                  
                  About TCF agent 
                  
                

The TCF agent is a software included in the Petalinux distribution by default which acts as a bridge between the PC and the board. This allows transferring, running, and debugging applications through Ethernet, removing the need for connecting a JTAG cable.
For more information about the TCF agent, go to AMD Docs.




In the Main tab:

Add a new Agent with the IP address of the Genesys 2 board (40.0.0.2).
Click on Test Connection and ensure that it successfully establishes connection.




In the Application tab:

Change the working directory to /home/petalinux.
Change the remote directory to /home/petalinux/<name-of-the-.elf>.



Click Apply and then Run.


The Output terminal of the application should automatically open below and display the Hello World.




                  
                  How does the application work? 
                  
                

The message Hello World is printed by the application by the board to stdout using printf, then the TCF agent connects stdout of the application to the PC, so the message is displayed on the PC.




                  
                  Exercise 1 
                  
                

Code an application that takes the value of the switches through the GPIOs and puts it directly into the LEDs above the switches in the Genesys 2 board.




                  
                  Exercise 2 
                  
                

Code an application that takes the value of the switches of one Genesys 2 board, sends it to other Genesys 2 board through ethernet and turns the LEDs of said board on. Use pthreads to create one thread that reads the switches and sends it to other board and another one that listens in a TCP socket for any message to turn on its own LEDs


Debugging applications in Vitis
Besides just running your application, you can also debug it step by step.
This is done in exactly the same way as when running normally your application, but instead of clicking on Run, click on Debug and the debugging interface should appear, breaking at the start of the main() function.


                  
                  Note
                  
                

You can put breakpoints by double-clicking on the lines of the editor, just like in any other editor.




                  
                  Warning
                  
                

In case your application is not entering debug mode, make sure to disable any optimization flag (-O0) and enable the debug information flag (-g3).



EXTRA: adding your software to a production image
If you have reached this section before the session ended, congratulations! This last section is dedicated to including your source code that you made on Vitis in the previous section inside the Petalinux image, so that it is readily available to anyone that flashes your OS image (the .mcs file) without having to transfer the application afterwards through Ethernet like we did.
Petalinux Tools is Yocto-based, meaning that it works through recipes. To add a new software component, we need to add a new recipe. The steps are as follows:

Create a new application recipe:
petalinux-create -t apps --template c -n my-gpio-app --enable

The new application source code should be created in project-spec/meta-user/recipes-apps/my-gpio-app. Inside this folder, there should be a .bb file and a folder called files. Open the files folder.
Copy all your source code files inside the files folder, erasing all the .c and .h files that were there, MAKING SURE THAT YOU LEAVE THE MAKEFILE.
Open the aforementioned .bb file. This file contains a list of all the files that will be used to build your application in the form of an environment variable called SRC_URI.
You need to point all the files you included in the SRC_URI files using the URI format that is usually used in web browsers, so file://<file-name>. These files are taken with respect to the route of the files folder, so if the files are already in the files folder and not in any subdirectory, you just have to specify the file name.
After that, we can make a quick test to see if our application is building correctly. This can be done by using the petalinux-build command with a -c parameter which indicates that we only want to build a specific component of our system. The name that you put in the -c is the same as the one you used to create the application in step 1.
petalinux-build -c my-gpio-app

If the application built successfully, congratulations! You got your application included in the root file system of your Linux image. This means that if you make a new .mcs file after running petalinux-build, you will get this application directly included in your rootfs.

3_sesion_2_vitis_unified.title: EXTRA: Coding applications in Vitis Unified
3_sesion_2_vitis_unified.content:
Coding applications in Vitis Unified
AMD provides an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for their FPGA solutions, where all the tools needed for building and debugging software are included. This software is called Vitis and, from 2023.2 onwards, Vitis Unified IDE. This latest version of Vitis is based on Visual Studio Code interface but specially made for the AMD products.
In this section, you will code an application for the Microblaze CPU that you built in the previous session. The statement is as follows:
!!! abstract Application coding
Code an application that takes the value of the switches through the GPIOs and puts it directly into the LEDs above the switches in the Genesys 2 board
To code this application, please take a look into how GPIO works in Linux through sysfs.
The steps to perform are as follows:

Open Vitis 2024.1 Unified IDE
Select a folder where to create your workspace

Create a platform project by clicking in File -> New Component -> Platform

Write a name for your platform like microblaze_platform
Click Next and select the same XSA that you used for building the Petalinux project.
Click Next and select linux in the operating system section. Click Next and finish.
 
Build the platform project by clicking on the Build button
 
Create an application project by using the Hello World Example of Linux. There is an icon in the side bar of Vitis that shows a list of possible examples to start your application. The one we are going to choose is Linux Hello World.



Select the built platform in the Select Platform screen.

!!! warning
If the platform you created is not shown, make sure that you clicked Build before creating the application component!
 
!!! info Suggestion
Try to run first the Hello world application before coding the application, to ensure that everything works.
10.   Build the application
11.   Connect through Ethernet the board
12.   Assign 40.0.0.1 to the PC statically

13.   Assign to the Genesys 2 Linux the 40.0.0.2 ip address by using the following command:
sudo ifconfig eth0 40.0.0.2
You can check if the IP was correctly assigned by typing the ifconfig command.
It should be like the screenshoot below:
 
14.   Click on the cog wheel next to the Run button to open the Run settings:
  
15.   Click on the New button in the Target connection. This is done to create a new connection through the ethernet link to the board
 
16.   Input the IP address of the board, so 40.0.2. 
17.   Click on Test Connection button BEFORE clicking OK. There should be a message like this one. If there is not, the run button will not work. 
 

Click on the run button. The application should be running. If you run the Hellow World, there should a printed message in the debug console below.

index.title: Home
index.content:
Welcome to the laboratory sessions of embedded Linux in Firmware Development on OS
This is the main index for the lab sessions that will be done in the next two days.
Sessions

Session 1: Microblaze Platform for Petalinux
Session 2: Software Components of Petalinux

Additional documentation

Extra: Coding in Vitis Unified

Feel free to navigate through the sessions to learn more about building and working with Petalinux systems.
